Chapter 1 — Executive Overview 
Chapter 1 – Executive Overview 
Artificial intelligence is transforming how organisations interact with customers, process information, automate decisions, and manage 
complex operational workflows. However, enterprise systems require much more than intelligent responses. They must remain 
reliable, secure, scalable, auditable, and capable of operating continuously under changing business conditi

However, enterprise systems require much more than intelligent responses. They must remain 
reliable, secure, scalable, auditable, and capable of operating continuously under changing business conditions. This is particularly 
important in the travel industry, where customer requests must be translated into real-world actions involving external providers, 
financial transactions, operational constraints, regulatory requirements, and constantly changing information. 
The Enterprise AI Flight Booking Platform presented in this document has been designed as a complete enterprise operational system 
that incorporates artificial intelligence as one of its capabilities rather than treating AI as the entire solution. The architecture combines 
stateful workflow orchestration, advanced retrieval-augmented generation, knowledge engineering, graph-based reasoning, external 
system integration, event-driven processing, reliability engineering, cloud-native deployment, observability, governance, and 
continuous AI lifecycle management. 
The platform enables travellers to search for flights, compare alternatives, understand airline policies, evaluate costs, receive 
recommendations, complete bo

nd 
continuous AI lifecycle management. 
The platform enables travellers to search for flights, compare alternatives, understand airline policies, evaluate costs, receive 
recommendations, complete bookings, process payments, and manage travel activities throughout the booking lifecycle. At the same 
time, it supports enterprise requirements such as operational resilience, auditability, compliance, scalability, fault tolerance, and 
continuous improvement. 
The architecture is built upon several core principles. Business workflows drive architectural decisions rather than technology choices. 
Stateful orchestration manages complex multi-step journeys. Retrieval and knowledge engineering ensure that AI operates on 
accurate and up-to-date information. Deterministic execution controls all financial and operational transactions. Event-driven 
processing supports scalability and loose coupling between services. Reliability patterns protect the platform from failures. Cloud-
native deployment enables elasticity and operational efficiency. Governance and observability provide transparency and control 
across the entire lifecycle. 
To achieve these objectives, the platform combines multip

nt enables elasticity and operational efficiency. Governance and observability provide transparency and control 
across the entire lifecycle. 
To achieve these objectives, the platform combines multiple specialised technologies. LangGraph manages stateful workflow 
orchestration. LangChain provides reusable AI application components. LlamaIndex supports knowledge engineering and retrieval. 
GPT and Claude provide complementary reasoning capabilities. Hugging Face supplies open-source models, embeddings, rerankers, 
and experimentation capabilities. LangSmith provides tracing and observability across AI workflows, while TruLens evaluates answer 
quality, groundedness, relevance, and hallucination risk. 
The architecture has been organised into seven complementary views. The first view presents the enterprise methodology used to 
design AI systems. The second view presents the complete flight-booking platform architecture. The third view illustrates the end-to-
end operational flow from user request to final response. The fourth view focuses on knowledge engineering and retrieval. The fifth 
view describes runtime deployment and scaling. The sixth view addresses reliability and consi

user request to final response. The fourth view focuses on knowledge engineering and retrieval. The fifth 
view describes runtime deployment and scaling. The sixth view addresses reliability and consistency. The seventh view introduces the 
enterprise LLMOps lifecycle that governs prompts, agents, models, retrieval systems, evaluation, deployment, and continuous 
improvement. 
Together, these views provide a complete blueprint for designing, deploying, operating, governing, and evolving enterprise AI systems. 
Although the platform is presented using a flight-booking scenario, the methodology and architectural principles can be applied to 
many other domains including healthcare, financial services, public-sector services, predictive maintenance, logistics, customer 
support, and enterprise knowledge systems. 
The objective of this document is therefore not simply to describe a flight-booking application, but to demonstrate how modern 
enterprise AI systems can be engineered in a structured, governed, scalable, and production-ready manner.

Chapter 2 — Enterprise AI Architecture Design Methodology (Diagram A) 
Chapter 2 – Enterprise AI Architecture Design Methodology 
Diagram A presents the design methodology used to construct the Enterprise AI Flight Booking Platform. Rather than starting with 
specific technologies, cloud providers, databases, or language models, the methodology begins with a sequence of architectural 
decisions that progressively transform a business requirement into a production-ready AI system. 
The process begins with understanding the business workflow. Every enterprise system exists to support a business objective, and 
therefore the first responsibility of the architect is to understand the end-to-end journey that the organisation wishes to support. In 
the flight-booking domain, this journey includes flight discovery, comparison of alternatives, policy interpretation, recommendation 
generation, payment processing, booking confirmation, customer communication, and ongoing travel support. Understanding this 
workflow establishes the foundation for every subsequent architectural decision. 
Once the workflow has been defined, the next q

customer communication, and ongoing travel support. Understanding this 
workflow establishes the foundation for every subsequent architectural decision. 
Once the workflow has been defined, the next question concerns state. The architect must determine whether the system needs to 
remember information across interactions. Flight booking is inherently stateful because the platform must preserve passenger details, 
selected itineraries, payment status, booking progress, preferences, confirmations, and workflow checkpoints. This decision 


[Page 6]
immediately influences storage architecture because state must be preserved in durable systems rather than inside language models 
or application instances. 
After establishing state requirements, attention shifts to storage strategy. Different types of information require different storage 
mechanisms. Operational transactions are typically stored in relational databases, session information may reside within distributed 
caches, workflow checkpoints may be maintained in dedicated state stores, and retrieval assets may be maintained within vector and 
graph databases. Selecting the correct storage strategy ensures consistency, performance

ckpoints may be maintained in dedicated state stores, and retrieval assets may be maintained within vector and 
graph databases. Selecting the correct storage strategy ensures consistency, performance, and durability throughout the platform. 
With state and storage defined, the architecture addresses decision logic. Some decisions can be implemented using deterministic 
business rules, while others require probabilistic reasoning and contextual interpretation. Enterprise AI systems frequently combine 
both approaches. Artificial intelligence supports interpretation, recommendation, ranking, explanation, and planning, while 
deterministic logic controls validation, compliance, workflow transitions, financial operations, and business policy enforcement. 
The next phase introduces knowledge engineering. Before any retrieval system can provide useful information, knowledge must be 
collected, organised, transformed, indexed, validated, and maintained. Knowledge engineering converts enterprise information into 
AI-ready assets that can later support retrieval and reasoning. This includes documents, policies, operational feeds, regulatory 
information, support materials, and structured e

ise information into 
AI-ready assets that can later support retrieval and reasoning. This includes documents, policies, operational feeds, regulatory 
information, support materials, and structured enterprise data. 
Once knowledge has been prepared, the architecture defines the retrieval strategy. Different business domains require different 
retrieval approaches. Hybrid retrieval combines semantic and keyword search. Contextual retrieval incorporates workflow state and 
conversation history. Agentic retrieval introduces planning and tool selection. Graph retrieval exploits relationships between 
interconnected entities. The chosen strategy determines how knowledge will be accessed during runtime. 
The methodology then moves into tooling strategy. Modern enterprise systems rarely operate in isolation. They interact with external 
APIs, operational services, payment gateways, customer databases, identity systems, notification platforms, and third-party providers. 
Identifying and governing these integrations is essential because they become the primary source of operational truth within the 
system. 
Once external dependencies have been identified, the architecture determines how e

ning these integrations is essential because they become the primary source of operational truth within the 
system. 
Once external dependencies have been identified, the architecture determines how execution should occur. Some operations require 
immediate responses and therefore execute synchronously. Others can occur independently in the background and therefore execute 
asynchronously. Establishing this separation early helps optimise responsiveness, scalability, and reliability. 
The next consideration concerns work triggering. Activities may be initiated by direct user requests, queued tasks, or business events. 
Request-driven workflows typically support interactive user journeys, while queue-driven and event-driven workflows support 
asynchronous processing and large-scale distributed operations. 
After execution patterns have been defined, reliability becomes a primary concern. Distributed systems inevitably encounter failures 
involving networks, services, providers, databases, and infrastructure. Reliability strategies therefore introduce retries, dead-letter 
queues, idempotency controls, reconciliation processes, fallback mechanisms, timeout policies, and circuit break

s, and infrastructure. Reliability strategies therefore introduce retries, dead-letter 
queues, idempotency controls, reconciliation processes, fallback mechanisms, timeout policies, and circuit breakers. These patterns 
ensure that temporary failures do not compromise business outcomes. 
The methodology then addresses scaling. Different architectural components require different scaling strategies. Stateless services 
often scale horizontally through replication, while stateful systems require specialised scaling approaches involving partitioning, 
caching, replication, and workload distribution. Autoscaling policies ensure that resources expand and contract according to demand. 
Once scaling requirements have been identified, runtime architecture can be defined. This includes compute environments, container 
platforms, networking, service communication, storage infrastructure, runtime isolation, and operational services. The runtime 
environment transforms architectural designs into executable production systems. 
Observability is introduced next because operating complex systems requires visibility into behaviour and performance. Metrics, logs, 
traces, dashboards, alerts, and t

executable production systems. 
Observability is introduced next because operating complex systems requires visibility into behaviour and performance. Metrics, logs, 
traces, dashboards, alerts, and telemetry provide the information required to understand how the system behaves and to diagnose 
issues rapidly when problems occur. 
Governance follows naturally from observability. Enterprise systems must remain secure, compliant, auditable, and trustworthy. 
Governance encompasses identity management, access control, tenant isolation, secrets management, privacy controls, compliance 
requirements, audit trails, and responsible AI practices. Governance is embedded throughout the architecture rather than being 
treated as an isolated capability. 
Deployment represents the final operational stage. Continuous integration and continuous delivery pipelines automate testing, 
validation, packaging, deployment, rollback, and release management. Infrastructure, applications, models, prompts, retrieval assets, 
and policies are all managed through controlled deployment processes that reduce operational risk. 
The methodology concludes with the LLMOps lifecycle. Modern AI systems require contin

ieval assets, 
and policies are all managed through controlled deployment processes that reduce operational risk. 
The methodology concludes with the LLMOps lifecycle. Modern AI systems require continuous management of prompts, agents, 
models, retrieval configurations, embeddings, evaluation datasets, workflows, and operational metrics. LLMOps ensures that AI assets 
are versioned, tested, monitored, governed, and continuously improved throughout their lifecycle. 
The result of this methodology is a production-ready enterprise AI system that remains scalable, reliable, observable, governed, 
auditable, and maintainable. More importantly, it provides a repeatable framework that can be applied consistently across different 
domains and use cases. While the flight-booking platform serves as the reference implementation throughout this document, the 
methodology itself forms a general blueprint for designing and operating enterprise AI systems at scale.

Chapter 3 – Enterprise AI Flight Booking Platform Architecture 
Having established the design methodology in the previous chapter, the next step is to apply those principles to the flight booking 
domain. Diagram B presents the complete Enterprise AI Flight Booking Platform Architecture and shows how all major business, AI, 
data, integration, operational, and governance capabilities work together as a single production-ready system. 
The architecture follows a layered design approach in which each layer has a clear responsibility while remaining loosely coupled from 
the others. This separation improves maintainability, scalability, reliability, and governance while allowing individual components to 
evolve independently over time.

The platform begins with the user channels. Travellers may interact through a web application, mobile application, chat interface, 
voice assistant, email service, travel agent portal, partner APIs, or other digital touchpoints. Regardless of the channel used, every 
request enters the platform through a common access layer that ensures consistent validation, security, monitoring, and routing. 
Before requests reach the AI services, they pass through the global traffic management layer. This layer provides DNS routing, 
geographic routing, distributed denial-of-service protection, web application firewall capabilities, and traffic analytics. The objective 
is to ensure that requests are routed efficiently and securely while protecting the platform from malicious activity and large-scale 
traffic fluctuations. 
Requests then enter the API Gateway layer, which serves as the formal entry point into the platform. The gateway performs 
authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, request validation, audit loggin

equests then enter the API Gateway layer, which serves as the formal entry point into the platform. The gateway performs 
authentication, authorisation, rate limiting, request validation, audit logging, and schema validation. It acts as a protective boundary 
between external users and internal services, ensuring that only valid and authorised requests are processed further. 
Once requests have been validated, they are forwarded to the AI orchestration and agent platform, which forms the core intelligence 
layer of the solution. This layer is built around LangGraph and is responsible for managing complex workflows that require state, 
memory, coordination, and decision making across multiple steps. 
At the centre of this layer is the workflow orchestrator. The orchestrator manages session state, workflow state, conversation context, 
guardrails, policies, and decision routing. Unlike simple request-response systems, flight booking involves multiple stages that may 
span several minutes or even longer. The platform must remember user preferences, selected flights, payment status, itinerary 
information, and workflow checkpoints throughout the journey. The orchestrator therefore acts

stateful interactions. 
Above the orchestration engine sit specialised agents that perform distinct business functions. Rather than relying on a single general-
purpose assistant, the architecture adopts a multi-agent approach. Dedicated agents support flight search, booking management, 
payments, itinerary generation, refunds, customer support, policy interpretation, and loyalty services. Each agent focuses on a specific 
business capability while remaining coordinated through the orchestration layer. 
To support intelligent decision making, the platform includes a dedicated retrieval and knowledge layer powered by LlamaIndex. This 
layer enables the system to access enterprise knowledge rather than relying solely on the language model's internal training data.

Retrieval engines support Hybrid RAG, Contextual RAG, Agentic RAG, and Graph RAG depending on the nature of the query. 
Hybrid retrieval combines semantic and keyword search to maximise retrieval accuracy. Contextual retrieval incorporates user 
preferences, session information, and workflow state. Agentic retrieval allows the system to plan retrieval strategies and select 
appropriate tools. Graph retrieval exploits relationships between entities such as airports, airlines, routes, countries, regulations, and 
travel restrictions. 
The retrieval layer includes specialised retrievers, rerankers, and metadata filters. Vector retrievers support semantic similarity search, 
sparse retrievers support keyword matching, graph retrievers explore entity relationships, and reranking models improve result quality 
before information is supplied to the reasoning layer.

regulations, travel advisories, operational flight data, airport information, customer support documentation, loyalty programmes, and

Supporting these operational layers is the event and streaming backbone. Modern enterprise systems cannot rely entirely on 
synchronous communication because many activities occur independently of the user interaction. Events therefore provide loose 
coupling between services and enable scalable asynchronous processing. 
The platform uses technologies such as Apache Kafka, Azure Event Hubs, or Amazon Kinesis to transport business events throughout 
the ecosystem. Examples include booking events, payment events, itinerary events, user events, notification events, and operational 
events. These event streams allow independent services to react to business activities without introducing tight dependencies 
between systems. 
Event processing is handled by the worker and consumer layer. Consumers subscribe to event streams and continuously process 
incoming events. Workers execute asynchronous business tasks such as payment processing, itinerary generation, graph updates, 
notification delivery, analytics processing, reconciliation, and retry handling. This separation allows synchronous user interactions to 
remain responsive w

and backups. Search indexes support full-text and hybrid search. Time-series databases store telemetry and monitoring information. 
Data lakes provide long-term analytical storage for reporting and machine learning activities.

The previous chapters described the overall architecture of the platform and the runtime flow that transforms a traveller's request 
into a completed booking. However, an important question still remains. How does the system obtain the knowledge required to 
answer questions, interpret policies, compare options, explain restrictions, and provide trustworthy recommendations? 
The answer lies in knowledge engineering. 
Knowledge engineering is one of the most important aspects of modern enterprise AI systems because language models alone do not 
possess reliable access to current business information. Airline policies change regularly. Refund rules evolve. Travel advisories

and disappear. Airport information changes. Loyalty programmes are updated. Flight schedules are continuously revised. 
Consequently, relying solely on the knowledge embedded within a language model would quickly lead to outdated, incomplete, or 
incorrect responses. 
Knowledge engineering solves this problem by transforming enterprise information into AI-ready assets that can be retrieved and used 
during runtime. Diagram D presents the complete lifecycle through which raw information becomes searchable, retrievable, traceable, 
governed, and usable by the flight booking platform. 
The lifecycle begins with knowledge acquisition. Enterprise information originates from many different sources and formats. Some 
information exists as documents such as airline policies, baggage rules, refund conditions, travel regulations, and support manuals. 
Other information is stored in databases, operational systems, APIs, ticketing systems, web portals, or third-party services. Additional

Because these sources differ significantly in structure and format, they must first be ingested into a controlled knowledge pipeline. 
Connectors, APIs, file loaders, web crawlers, streaming interfaces, and change-data-capture mechanisms collect information and 
transfer it into the enterprise knowledge platform. The objective is to establish a consistent and repeatable method for acquiring 
information regardless of its origin. 
Once information has been collected, parsing and extraction activities begin. Raw content is rarely suitable for retrieval in its original 
form. Documents may contain tables, images, headers, footers, inconsistent formatting, duplicated information, or irrelevant content.

During this stage, text extraction processes convert documents into machine-readable content. Metadata extraction identifies

category. Entity extraction identifies important concepts such as airlines, airports, destinations, countries, travel restrictions, baggage 
allowances, refund policies, loyalty programmes, and operational procedures. Additional processing may identify key-value pairs,

Once information has been extracted, it must be organised into units that can be retrieved efficiently. This process is known as

In the flight booking platform, semantic chunking forms the primary strategy. Airline policies, refund conditions, baggage rules, and 
travel advisories often contain related information that should remain together. Splitting such information arbitrarily may destroy 
important context and reduce retrieval accuracy. Semantic chunking therefore attempts to preserve logical meaning boundaries rather 
than relying solely on fixed character counts. 
Section-based chunking provides an additional layer of organisation. Airline documents naturally contain sections describing baggage 
rules, cancellation policies, check-in requirements, special assistance procedures, and loyalty programmes. Preserving these 
boundaries improves retrieval precision because complete policy sections can be retrieved when required. 
For particularly large documents, recursive chunking and sliding-window chunking may also be employed. These techniques balance 
retrieval granularity with contextual completeness, ensuring that important information is neither fragmented excessively nor

Platform. While previous diagrams focused on business capabilities and logical architecture, this chapter focuses on the operational 
infrastructure required to execute those capabilities in production. 
The platform is designed as a cloud-native distributed system. Rather than deploying all functionality within a single application server, 
the architecture separates responsibilities across multiple independently deployable services. This approach improves scalability, 
resilience, maintainability, fault isolation, deployment flexibility, and operational efficiency.

Incoming requests first enter the platform through the global traffic management layer. Load balancers distribute traffic across

Diagram F presents the reliability and consistency architecture of the Enterprise AI Flight Booking Platform. Its objective is to ensure 
that business operations remain trustworthy even when individual components fail. Rather than assuming failures will never occur, 
the architecture assumes that failures are inevitable and provides mechanisms to detect, isolate, recover from, and compensate for 
those failures. 
The first principle of the reliability architecture is that failures should be expected rather than treated as exceptional events. Every 
external dependency introduces uncertainty. Flight providers may respond slowly. Payment gateways may become temporarily

The reliability journey begins whenever a service invokes another service. Each interaction is protected by timeout policies that define 
how long the caller is willing to wait for a response. Without timeouts, requests could remain indefinitely blocked while waiting for 
unavailable systems. Timeouts therefore provide the first layer of protection by preventing resource exhaustion and ensuring that 
failures are detected promptly. 
When a timeout or temporary failure occurs, the platform generally attempts recovery through retries. Not all failures are permanent. 
Network congestion, brief service interruptions, and transient provider issues often resolve themselves within seconds. Retry 
mechanisms therefore attempt the operation again after a controlled delay. Exponential backoff strategies increase waiting periods 
between retry attempts, reducing pressure on already stressed systems while improving the probability of successful recov

However, retries alone are insufficient. If an external provider becomes unavailable for an extended period, repeated retries may 
simply increase system load and amplify the problem. To prevent this behaviour, the architecture introduces circuit breakers. Circuit 
breakers continuously monitor service health and temporarily stop requests from reaching failing dependencies. When failure rates 
exceed predefined thresholds, the circuit opens and blocks further requests. After a recovery period, the system gradually reintroduces 
traffic and evaluates whether the dependency has recovered. This approach protects both the platform and external providers from 
cascading failures.

To address these scenarios, the platform employs reconciliation mechanisms. Reconciliation services compare records across multiple

compared against internal databases. Loyalty updates may be compared against transactional history. Whenever discrepancies are 
detected, reconciliation workflows determine the appropriate corrective action.

Chapter 10 – Technology Selection and Architectural Justification 
 
 
Throughout the previous chapters, numerous technologies, frameworks, architectural patterns, and operational approaches have 
been introduced. However, enterprise architecture is not simply about identifying available technologies. It is about selecting 
technologies that best support business objectives, operational requirements, scalability goals, governance expectations, and long-
term maintainability. Every technology choice introduces benefits, limitations, costs, and trade-offs. Consequently, it is important to 
understand not only what was selected, but also why it was selected. 
This chapter explains the rationale behind the major technology d

This chapter explains the rationale behind the major technology decisions that underpin the Enterprise AI Flight Booking Platform and 
demonstrates how those choices align with the platform's functional and operational requirements. 
The first major architectural decision concerns workflow orchestration. Flight booking is inherently a multi-step process involving 
search, comparison, recommendation, validation, payment, booking, confirmation, and post-booking activities. These activities may 
span multiple user interactions and require persistent state throughout the journey. For this reason, the platform adopts LangGraph 
as its primary orchestration framework. 
LangGraph was selected because it provides explicit support for stateful workflows, workflow graphs, conditional routing, 
checkpointing, agent coordination, and long-running processes. These capabilities align closely with the requirements of flight booking. 
Traditional request-response architectures struggle to manage complex conversational workflows because they lack explicit state 
man

apabilities align closely with the requirements of flight booking. 
Traditional request-response architectures struggle to manage complex conversational workflows because they lack explicit state 
management. LangGraph provides a structured mechanism for managing state transitions while maintaining visibility and control over 
workflow execution. 
LangChain complements LangGraph rather than replacing it. Whereas LangGraph focuses on orchestration and workflow control, 
LangChain provides reusable components that simplify prompt execution, tool integration, retrieval integration, memory handling, 
output parsing, and model interaction. Together they provide a practical foundation for enterprise AI application development. 
LangGraph manages the workflow while LangChain supplies many of the building blocks used within that workflow. 
The next major decision concerns retrieval and knowledge engineering. Enterprise AI systems require access to current business 
knowledge rather than relying exclusively on pre-trained model knowledge. For this reason, retrieval-augmented generation forms a 
central component of the platform architecture. 
LlamaIndex was selected as the primary knowledge

g exclusively on pre-trained model knowledge. For this reason, retrieval-augmented generation forms a

LlamaIndex was selected as the primary knowledge engineering framework because it extends beyond simple retrieval. It supports 
ingestion, parsing, chunking, metadata extraction, embedding generation, indexing, retrieval orchestration, graph construction, and 
retrieval optimisation. This broader scope aligns closely with enterprise requirements because knowledge engineering begins long 
before retrieval occurs. The platform must first transform raw information into retrieval-ready assets before retrieval can become 
effective. 
The retrieval architecture itself adopts a combination of Hybrid RAG, Contextual RAG, Agentic RAG, and Graph RAG rather than relying 
on a single retrieval technique. Hybrid retrieval combines semantic search and keyword search to maximise retrieval precision. 


[Page 43]
Contextual retrieval incorporates workflow state, user preferences, and conversational history. Agentic retrieval allows the system to 
determine which retrieval actions should be performed. Graph retrieval exploits relationships between a

low state, user preferences, and conversational history. Agentic retrieval allows the system to 
determine which retrieval actions should be performed. Graph retrieval exploits relationships between airports, airlines, countries, 
restrictions, routes, and operational conditions. 
This combination was selected because no single retrieval strategy performs optimally across all scenarios. Policy questions benefit 
from hybrid retrieval. Personalised recommendations benefit from contextual retrieval. Complex planning tasks benefit from agentic 
retrieval. Relationship-oriented questions benefit from graph retrieval. By combining multiple retrieval strategies, the platform can 
adapt dynamically to the needs of each interaction. 
The platform also adopts a graph-based knowledge layer. Traditional vector retrieval excels at finding semantically similar content but 
often struggles to represent complex relationships explicitly. Flight booking frequently involves interconnected entities such as airports, 
airlines, routes, countries, regulations, weather conditions, and loyalty programmes. Graph databases provide a natural 
representation for these relationships.

Neo4j was identified as the preferred graph technology because of its maturity, extensive ecosystem, strong query capabilities, and 
widespread adoption within enterprise environments. Alternative graph platforms such as Amazon Neptune and Cosmos DB Gremlin 
remain viable options, particularly when alignment with specific cloud providers is desirable. However, Neo4j offers an excellent 
balance of functionality, usability, and operational maturity for graph-centric applications. 
The platform also requires vector storage for semantic retrieval. Several technologies were considered including FAISS, Pinecone, 
Qdrant, Weaviate, and Azure AI Search. FAISS provides excellent local indexing capabilities but requires additional infrastructure 
management. Pinecone offers a fully managed vector database with strong scalability characteristics. Qdrant provides flexible filtering 
and operational control. Weaviate combines vector retrieval with knowledge modelling features. Azure AI Search integrates semantic

For an enterprise flight booking platform, Azure AI Search combined with a dedicated vector store such as Pinecone or Qdrant provides 
an effective balance between operational simplicity, scalability, governance, and retrieval performance. The final choice depends on 
cloud strategy, deployment constraints, and operational preferences. 
The platform also requires foundation models capable of reasoning, explanation, summarisation, planning, and conversational 
interaction. Rather than committing to a single provider, the architecture intentionally supports multiple model ecosystems. 
GPT models were selected because of their strong reasoning capabilities, structured output generation, workflow support, and broad

e adoption. GPT performs particularly well when integrated with orchestration frameworks that require predictable outputs, 
tool coordination, and structured responses. Its ability to generate consistent JSON outputs and support workflow-driven applications 
makes it highly suitable for enterprise environments. 
Claude was selected because of its strengths in long-context reasoning, document interpretation, and conversational explanation. 
Airline policies, travel regulations, refund conditions, and support documentation often involve lengthy and complex content. Claude's 
ability to process large contexts makes it particularly valuable when analysing substantial documents or generating detailed 
explanations. 
Importantly, the architecture does not view GPT and Claude as competitors. Instead, they are treated as complementary capabilities. 
GPT may be preferred for workflow-oriented reasoning and structured outputs, while Claude may be preferred for document-centric 
reasoning and policy interpretation. Model routing policies allow the platform to select the most appropriate model for each task.

The platform also incorporates Hugging Face technologies. Hugging Face provides access to a vast ecosystem of open-source models, 
embeddings, rerankers, datasets, and experimentation tools. While GPT and Claude provide managed model services, Hugging Face 
provides flexibility, transparency, and customisation. 
Embeddings such as BGE and E5 may be sourced from the Hugging Face ecosystem. Cross-encoder rerankers can improve retrieval 
quality. Specialised domain models may support particular use cases. Fine-tuning capabilities enable organisations to customise

model ecosystem and reducing vendor dependency. 
The architecture also places significant emphasis on observability and evaluation. AI systems cannot be managed effectively without 
visibility into behaviour and quality. For this reason, LangSmith and TruLens were selected as core components of the operational 
ecosystem.

y into behaviour and quality. For this reason, LangSmith and TruLens were selected as core components of the operational

LangSmith focuses on tracing and observability. It records prompt execution, workflow transitions, tool invocations, retrieval 
operations, and model interactions. When unexpected behaviour occurs, LangSmith allows engineers to investigate the complete 
execution path and understand how decisions were made. 
TruLens focuses on evaluation. Metrics such as groundedness, answer relevance, context relevance, faithfulness, retrieval quality, and 
hallucination risk provide quantitative measures of AI performance. This capability is particularly important because enterprise AI 
systems must be evaluated continuously rather than relying solely on subjective user feedback. 
Together, LangSmith and TruLens provide complementary capabilities. LangSmith explains what happened, while TruLens evaluates 
how well it happened. 
The platform also adopts an event-driven architecture supported by technologies such as Kafka, Azure Event Hubs, or Amazon Kinesis.

The separation between consumers and workers further enhances scalability and maintainability. Consumers monitor event streams 
and coordinate processing. Workers perform business operations. This separation enables different workload categories to scale 
independently while maintaining clear operational boundaries. 
Cloud-native deployment represents another important architectural decision. Kubernetes was selected because it provides a mature 
platform for container orchestration, autoscaling, workload isolation, deployment automation, and operational resilience. By treating 
application services as independently deployable workloads, Kubernetes enables the p

ner orchestration, autoscaling, workload isolation, deployment automation, and operational resilience. By treating 
application services as independently deployable workloads, Kubernetes enables the platform to scale individual components 
according to demand rather than scaling the entire system uniformly. 
The architecture also incorporates extensive reliability mechanisms including retries, circuit breakers, idempotency controls, dead-
letter queues, reconciliation services, and fallback providers. These patterns were selected because flight booking involves financial 
transactions and external dependencies where failures can have significant business consequences. Reliability therefore becomes a 
business requirement rather than merely a technical preference. 
Finally, the platform adopts a comprehensive LLMOps lifecycle encompassing PromptOps, AgentOps, RAGOps, ModelOps, 
EvaluationOps, GovernanceOps, and DeploymentOps. This lifecycle was selected because enterprise AI systems require continuous 
management throughout their operational lives. Models change, prompts evolve, knowledge grows, evaluation criteria improve, and 
business requirements shift. LLMOps provides the gover

uire continuous 
management throughout their operational lives. Models change, prompts evolve, knowledge grows, evaluation criteria improve, and

When viewed collectively, these technology choices reveal a consistent architectural philosophy. The platform favours explicit 
orchestration over hidden behaviour, governed execution over uncontrolled automation, retrieval over model memorisation, 
observability over opacity, resilience over optimism, and continuous improvement over static deployment. Each technology was 
selected not because it is fashionable or widely discussed, but because it contributes directly to the business, operational, and 
governance objectives of the platform. 
The resulting architecture combines the strengths of modern AI frameworks, retrieval technologies, cloud-native infrastructure, event-

The most important architectural decision we froze is: 
The LLM does not own the business workflow. The orchestrator owns the workflow. The retrieval layer owns knowledge access. External systems own operational 
truth. Deterministic services own transactions. The LLM provides reasoning, explanation, ranking, summarisation, and user interaction. 
This principle is what makes the architecture enterprise-grade, scalable, auditable, reliable, and suitable for a production flight booking platform.
